checks.a_analysis: The output contains sections and paragraphs that directly correspond to the text provided in the source PDF and its associated OCR. There are no invented section headings, hallucinated body text, or fabricated introductions. Every paragraph in the JSON has a clear basis in the original document.

The source document does not contain explicit contact information such as phone numbers or email addresses. The JSON faithfully reflects this by not including any contact info. URLs like 'NC.gov' appear in the text as part of descriptions of accomplishments, which is correct.

There are no tables in the source document. The various lists (What We Do, Boards and Commissions, Quick Facts, Priorities, and Accomplishments) have been correctly identified as lists in the JSON and contain the exact number of items found in the source.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: Each section is complete. Comparing the source OCR and the JSON, no key paragraphs or significant blocks of text are missing within any section. The linearized reading order is logical and preserves all information.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text in the JSON matches the provided OCR and the source PDF closely. One minor OCR discrepancy was noted: in Quick Facts, '600' appears as '6,00', but this matches the provided OCR stream for page 1 and is a minor character error that does not fundamentally alter the semantic meaning for an auditor or government employee. The conversion is highly faithful.

checks.a_analysis: The document's content appears uniquely in the output. Each section heading (MISSION, FUNDING, QUICK FACTS, etc.) and their respective text blocks are present only once, matching the source layout across two pages. There is no evidence of paragraphs or list items being repeated within the structured output.

The heading hierarchy is logical and consistent. The document uses H1 for the main title of each page ('N.C. Department of Information Technology' on page 1 and '2022 ACCOMPLISHMENTS' on page 2). Major sections on page 1 (MISSION, FUNDING, QUICK FACTS, etc.) are correctly assigned as H2. There are no skipped levels or nested errors.
The source document does not contain any tables; therefore, no table structure corruption is possible.

The multi-column layout on page 1 is linearized in a logical top-to-bottom, left-to-right fashion. The output processes the left column (Mission, Funding, Quick Facts) before the right column (What We Do, Boards and Commissions, Budget, Vision and Priorities). This maintains the thematic grouping of the source document and provides a clear, logical flow.

All bulleted sections in the source ('QUICK FACTS', 'WHAT WE DO', 'BOARDS AND COMMISSIONS', and 'priorities') have been faithfully converted into unordered lists with the correct number of items and appropriate nesting. The accomplishments on page 2, which appear as separate blocks in the source, have also been grouped into a list, which is a valid structural interpretation that preserves meaning.
The source document does not contain any active hyperlinks. The output does not inject any fabricated links; mentions of entities like 'NC.gov' remain as plain text, matching the source.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML rendering successfully transitions the multi-column layout of the source PDF into a single-column responsive layout. The logical reading order is preserved: the content from the first column of the PDF (Mission, Funding, Quick Facts) is presented first, followed by the content from the second column (What We Do, Boards, Budget, Vision). This is an expected transformation for accessible HTML and does not constitute a failure of organization.
There are no tables in the source document. The structured lists and data points are rendered clearly as lists or paragraphs in the HTML output.

The source PDF contains no meaningful images (photos, charts, or logos). The decorative pattern at the bottom of page 2 in the PDF is intentionally omitted in the HTML for accessibility compliance, which is not a defect.

There is no genuine body content duplication. The headings appear in both the 'Contents' navigation block and the main body, which is a standard accessibility feature and specifically excluded from being a defect. The actual text and data points appear only once in the document body.
